transcript:
[doctor] hey angela how are you
[patient] i'm good how are you
[doctor] i'm good so i see we are here you had a recent diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer and so we are following up on your neo adevant chemotherapy so you can you tell me how you're doing today
[patient] yeah you know i'm i'm doing okay i have a little bit of some shortness of breath that i noticed over the weekend and you know i i'm i considered myself pretty active before this i would walk my dog around the block and now i i ca n't even walk you know down the street without being short of breath
[doctor] okay yeah yeah i mean that's that's definitely understandable it says you had a four point four centimeter left upper lobe nodule in there so it's kinda big but that's understandable and we're doing the chemo to try to reduce that that that tumor that you do have so how often do you normally run with your dogs
[patient] i mean i would i would take them for i would normally take them for like mile long walks everyday and just this weekend i could only walk down to the end of the street i had to have my husband take the dog fit walk
[doctor] man okay what kind of dog do you have
[patient] i have a dobrman
[doctor] yeah they are pretty active that makes sense alright so have you been coughing have you had any phlegm
[patient] i've been having a dry cough no phlegm
[doctor] okay have you noticed any fever
[patient] no fever
[doctor] no fever alright what about any other symptoms like any body aches any sore throat sinus pain
[patient] well i mean now that you mentioned it like every time i swallow it hurts it does n't feel like i have a cold because it hurts down here more in my chest but every time i swallow it it's really painful
[doctor] okay so when you are swallowing i know some of my patients feel this do you feel like the food is getting stuck
[patient] no the food is not getting stuck it just hurts
[doctor] okay what about when you're you're taking liquids are you able to take in both hydrocort liquids
[patient] i mean i definitely feel like over the weekend i have n't been taking it as much because it hurt so much but i i am able to i am able to drink and i've been taking ensure and things like that i had a milk shake
[doctor] okay that's that's that's that's good yeah we we definitely want you to to take in as much liquid as possible best especially since you're on the chemotherapy and the radiation just so you wo n't get dehydrated because that will make you feel a lot worse if you are dehydrated okay so on a on a mental emotional standpoint i know you said you have your husband with you how you doing with that you have the support that you need you need to speak to me though
[patient] it's very helpful you know as you can understand i'm just like devastated when i got this diagnosis and i'm just i'm just trying to figure everything out and but he is very good and my daughter is is very helpful too and i you know i think for the most part i'm very fortunate to have them
[doctor] okay so how how are you feeling on your chemo see you're on a combination of cisplatin and etoposide you had your last dose a few days ago any nausea any fatigue from that
[patient] i was a little nauseated and tired but i took the zofran that you prescribed and that seemed to help and you know i i got some rest it's it's it's been okay
[doctor] okay that's good so let me do a quick physical exam on you just gon na check your vitals here so your vitals are normal your pulse ox is okay so everything's good with that so i'm gon na feel your neck on your neck exam i do n't appreciate any cervical lymphadenopathy no subclavicular adenopathy your heart exam regular rate and rhythm no murmur your lung exam so i do notice some crackles in your lungs bilaterally that's just a faint sound we hear when you take a deep breath so that could mean you have some fluid in there and that is to be expected due to your lung cancer diagnosis so i'm gon na look at your skin exam so on your skin exam on your chest i do see some erythema on the anterior aspect of your chest on the left side and that could be due to that radiation sometimes patients do get a radiation burn and it's it'll get better with time especially once you're done with the the radiation treatment and on your extremity it there is no lower extremity edema in your legs so prior to coming to see me you did get a chest x-ray it looks like you do have mild radiation pneumonitis that means you have inflammation of your lungs due to the radiation so let's talk a little bit about my assessment and plan for you so your first diagnosis you know is is the lung cancer so we are gon na continue you on that chemo regimen on that you do have continue on that that current radiation doses i think you're on forty five gray yep you're on that and then when the when the regimen's complete we'll do some more testing and hopefully we can see a resection of that tumor so that has just gotten little smaller and we can go inside and and take it out so for your second diagnosis the radiation pneumonitis i'm gon na prescribe you a low dose steroid prednisone forty milligrams one tablet a day for five days have you been on on a steroids before
[patient] no i've only ever heard bad things about them they make people jittery and gain weight
[doctor] i mean and and i for you that's that that's for patients that you know who are on them for a a long period of time right you're only gon na be on it for five days that should n't affect you too bad you might feel when you coming off of it after the five days a few muscle aches other than that you should be fine you should n't really experience much weight gain other other than not not much at all since you're only on on it for five days so but that's gon na help you reduce some of the inflammation in your lungs help you breathe better so we can continue the radiation treatment because we definitely need to do that because our most important thing right now is to try to decrease the size of that tumor okay and then for your third issue the oddy ophagia or it come terms the painful swallowing right you said you had that from the from the radiation so that's from the inflammation of your lungs that's kinda creeping up to your esophagus that's why you're you're feeling that pain so i'm gon na prescribe you a lidocaine squishy and swallow you can do that four times a day and so you'll do it and you'll be able to eat immediately afterwards because it would do some of that pain free from swallowing and that will help you taking liquids and food because we definitely need you well off it and and and hydrated while you're going through this cancer therapy okay
[patient] okay
[doctor] alright so do you have any questions for me
[patient] no
[doctor] alright so we will get you those medications those prescriptions and then we will see you back in two weeks
[patient] okay great thank you
[doctor] thanks
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up of stage III non-small cell lung cancer.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports recent diagnosis of stage III non-small cell lung cancer.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Patient reports she was previously active taking mile long walks with her doberman. She states her husband is her support system and has been helpful with this new diagnosis. Her daughter has also been helpful.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports receiving chemotherapy consisting of Cisplatin and etoposide. She is also taking Zofran.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports fatigue. Denies fever.
HENT: Reports odynophagia.
Respiratory: Reports dyspnea and dry cough.
Gastrointestinal: Reports nausea.

VITALS

Vitals are normal including pulse oximetry.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Neck
- General Examination: Neck is supple without cervical lymphadenopathy, or subclavicular adenopathy.

Respiratory
- Auscultation of Lungs: Rales in the lungs bilaterally.

Cardiovascular
- Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Musculoskeletal
- Examination: No edema.

Integumentary
- Examination: Some erythema on the left anterior aspect of the chest.

Hematology/Lymphatic/Immunology
- Palpation: No enlarged lymph nodes.

RESULTS

Pathology report is reviewed and shows evidence of a 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule.

Chest x-ray is reviewed and shows evidence of mild radiation pneumonitis.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage III non-small cell lung cancer.
- Medical Reasoning: This is a new diagnosis for this patient. Recent pathology report showed evidence of a 4.4 cm left upper lobe nodule.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed that once her treatment regimen is complete we will proceed with further testing and hopefully tumor resection.
- Medical Treatment: Patient will continue on current regimen consisting of Cisplatin and etoposide. Her radiation will remain at 45 Gy.

2. Radiation pneumonitis.
- Medical Reasoning: Recent chest x-ray showed evidence of mild radiation pneumonitis.
- Patient Education and Counseling: We discussed she is experiencing inflammation of the lungs secondary to her radiation treatment. The risks and benefits of prednisone were also discussed.
- Medical treatment: A prescription for prednisone 40 mg 1 tablet a day for 5 days was provided.

3. Odynophagia.
- Medical Reasoning: Patient reports symptoms of painful swallowing.
- Patient Education and Counseling: I advised her that her radiation pneumonitis is also affecting her esophagus which is resulting in her painful swallowing. We discussed the importance of nutrition and hydration while undergoing chemotherapy.
- Medical treatment: Prescription for lidocaine viscous solution to be completed 4 times a day was provided.
Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

INSTRUCTIONS

Patient will return for follow-up in 2 weeks.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old female, presents with shortness of breath that started over the weekend, which has progressed to the point where she can only walk a short distance without experiencing shortness of breath. She reports that she was previously an active person who would walk her dog a mile long every day. She denies coughing, phlegm, fever, body aches, sore throat, and sinus pain, but reports that swallowing is painful, particularly in her chest, and that she has not been taking her prescribed hydrocortisone liquids as much as instructed due to pain. The patient has a husband and daughter who provide support.

Past medical history: The patient has a recent diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer, for which she is undergoing neo-Adjuvant chemotherapy with cisplatin and etoposide. She has been experiencing nausea and fatigue from the chemotherapy, but reports that zofran has helped alleviate her nausea.

Review of systems: The patient reports dry cough, no phlegm, no fever, and no body aches or sore throat, but does report painful swallowing when swallowing.

Current medications: I prescribed zofran to alleviate nausea, hydrocortisone liquids for hydration, and Ensure and milkshakes for nutrition.

Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's pulse, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation are normal. Pulse ox is okay.

Physical exam: The patient's neck exam is unremarkable, with no cervical lymphadenopathy or subclavicular adenopathy. Her heart exam is regular, with no murmur. Her lung exam reveals crackles in both lungs, which could indicate fluid accumulation due to her lung cancer diagnosis. The skin exam reveals erythema on the anterior aspect of her chest on the left side, which could be due to radiation. There is no lower extremity edema.

Test Results: The patient had a chest x-ray, which showed mild radiation pneumonitis, or inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer, radiation pneumonitis, and odynophagia (painful swallowing) due to radiation.

Problem list: Lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; odynophagia; active; confirmed; suspected.

Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40 milligrams one tablet a day for five days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and alleviate symptoms. I also prescribed lidocaine spray for painful swallowing, to be taken four times a day. The patient should take this before eating and drinking to alleviate pain. I will get the patient these medications and see her back in two weeks.

Follow-up: We will see the patient in two weeks to monitor her progress and adjust her treatment plan as necessary.

Subjective:
The patient, a 45-year-old female, reports being okay, but experiencing shortness of breath, which started over the weekend, stating she was previously active and could walk her dog for a mile but now can only walk down the street without getting short of breath. She denies fever and has a dry cough but mentions that swallowing hurts, particularly in the chest area. She reports being able to drink liquids and take Ensure, but has decreased her intake due to pain. She is emotionally supported by her husband and daughter. She reports being a little nauseated and tired from chemotherapy but took Zofran and got rest, which helped. She has a Dobrahan dog.

Past medical history:
The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer, diagnosed recently, and is undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy with cisplatin and etoposide.

Review of systems:
The patient reports shortness of breath, dry cough, pain when swallowing, and decreased ability to drink liquids due to pain.

Current medications:
I prescribed Zofran as needed for nausea.

Objective:
Vital signs:
The patient's pulse ox is normal, and her other vitals are within normal limits.

Physical exam:
The patient has crackles in her lungs bilaterally, erythema on the anterior aspect of her chest on the left side, and no lower extremity edema in her legs. The cervical lymphadenopathy and subclavicular adenopathy are not appreciated.

Test Results:
The patient had a chest X-ray, which showed mild radiation pneumonitis.

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis, confirmed; and odynophagia, suspected.

Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40 milligrams daily for five days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and help with breathing. I also prescribed lidocaine spray to be used four times a day to alleviate pain when swallowing.

Follow-up:
The patient will see me back in two weeks.

Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 65-year-old female with a recent diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer, who is currently undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy. She reports shortness of breath, which began over the weekend, and has reduced her ability to walk her dog from a mile-long walk to a short walk down the street. She also reports a dry cough, no fever, and pain upon swallowing that is located in her chest, but not in her throat. She has been taking Ensure and other liquids, but has reduced her intake due to pain. She has been on a combination of cisplatin and etoposide chemotherapy, and has experienced some nausea and fatigue, which was managed with Zofran.

Past medical history: The patient has a diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer.

Review of systems: The patient reports shortness of breath, dry cough, pain upon swallowing, and some fatigue.

Current medications: I prescribed Zofran [dose not specified] [frequency not specified] for chemotherapy-induced nausea.

Objective:

Physical exam: The patient has a normal pulse, regular heart rate and rhythm, and no murmurs. The lung exam reveals bilateral crackles, which may indicate fluid in the lungs due to the lung cancer diagnosis. The skin exam shows erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which may be a radiation burn. There is no lower extremity edema in the patient's legs.

Test Results: The patient had a chest X-ray that showed mild radiation pneumonitis, which is inflammation of the lungs due to radiation.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer, radiation pneumonitis, and odynophagia (painful swallowing).

Problem list: Non-small cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; odynophagia.

Plan:
I prescribed prednisone 40mg one tablet a day for five days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and help the patient breathe better. I also prescribed lidocaine spray to be used four times a day to alleviate painful swallowing.

Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks to follow up on her treatment and to assess the effectiveness of the medications.

Subjective:

HPI: The patient reports experiencing shortness of breath, which started over the weekend, and is unable to walk her dog, a Doberman, for the usual mile-long walks due to fatigue. She describes the shortness of breath as a new symptom, which is different from her usual level of activity. She also reports a dry cough without phlegm and a painful sensation when swallowing, which hurts when she takes liquids. She denies fever, body aches, and sore throat. She has been taking Ensure and milkshakes to stay hydrated.

Review of systems: The patient reports difficulty swallowing and shortness of breath.

Current medications: I prescribed zofran for nausea and vomiting.

Her lung exam reveals crackles bilaterally, which may indicate fluid in the lungs due to her lung cancer diagnosis. Test Results: The patient's chest x-ray shows mild radiation pneumonitis, which is inflammation of the lungs due to the radiation.

Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient has stage three non-small cell lung cancer, radiation pneumonitis, and painful swallowing due to radiation.

Problem list: Non-small cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; painful swallowing.

Plan:

I prescribed prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and alleviate symptoms. I also prescribed lidocaine swish 4 times a day to alleviate pain when swallowing. I instructed the patient to continue her current chemotherapy regimen and radiation treatment.

Follow-up: The patient will be seen in two weeks for follow-up.

Subjective:

HPI: The patient is a 45-year-old female with a recent diagnosis of stage three non-small cell lung cancer, currently undergoing neo-adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation therapy. She reports shortness of breath, which has worsened over the weekend, stating she can no longer walk her dog without feeling short of breath, which is a significant change from her previous activity level. She also reports a dry cough, no phlegm, and a painful sensation when swallowing, which is worse when she swallows.

The lung exam reveals bilateral crackles, which could indicate fluid in the lungs due to the lung cancer diagnosis. The skin exam reveals erythema on the anterior aspect of the chest on the left side, which could be a radiation burn. There is no lower extremity edema in the legs.

Assessment / Problem List:

Problem list: Non-small cell lung cancer; radiation pneumonitis; painful swallowing.

Plan:

I will continue the patient on the current chemotherapy regimen and radiation doses. I will prescribe a low-dose steroid, prednisone 40mg daily for 5 days to reduce inflammation in the lungs and improve breathing. I will also prescribe a lidocaine spray to be used 4 times a day to alleviate painful swallowing.

Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for follow-up.
